content.text: Every autoregressive LLM decoding step appends a new key-vector and value-vector for every layer. By the time a 4096-token sequence completes, a Llama-3 70B model has produced ~80 GB of KV data per request. The original approach — pre-allocating a contiguous tensor of size
— wastes enormous amounts of GPU memory because most requests never reach the maximum length. PagedAttention, introduced in vLLM, borrows virtual-memory paging from OS design to eliminate this waste.
A contiguous KV cache has two fragmentation modes.
tokens but on average uses only 30–50% of that before generation finishes.

: after requests of different lengths complete, the freed contiguous regions cannot be reused for longer requests without defragmentation. Together these effects mean GPU utilization often sits below 50% even on a fully-loaded server.

attrs.alt: PagedAttention: Contiguous vs Paged KV Cache Memory Layout

content.text: Page Layout: Blocks and Block Tables
PagedAttention divides the KV cache into fixed-size
. Each block holds the K and V tensors for exactly
tokens across all layers. The default in vLLM is
. The shape of one physical block (for a single layer) is:
where 2 is for K and V,
is the number of KV heads (often fewer than Q heads in GQA), and
is the per-head dimension. vLLM allocates
of these blocks into a flat GPU tensor called the
maps logical block indices (0, 1, 2, …) to the physical block numbers assigned by the allocator. Given token at logical position
The block table itself is a small CPU-side integer array (one entry per logical block). At decode time vLLM copies it to GPU so the attention kernel can do the address translation.
Block Allocator: Free List and Ref Counting

The block allocator maintains a free list of physical block numbers. When a new token is generated and no partially-filled block exists for the sequence, the allocator pops one block from the free list and adds it to that sequence's block table. When a sequence finishes (or is preempted), all its blocks are returned to the free list. Here is a minimal Python model of this allocator:
At decode time a new query vector

) must attend to all previously generated K/V vectors. Because those vectors live in scattered physical blocks, the attention kernel takes the block table as an additional input and gathers blocks non-contiguously rather than assuming a flat buffer. The vLLM CUDA kernel does:
from GPU memory (small int32 array, one entry per logical block).
Apply online softmax across all blocks (same running-max technique as FlashAttention).
variant for long contexts). These are CUDA kernels; you rarely call them directly. The public interface is the

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

# vLLM manages the paged KV cache internally.
# gpu_memory_utilization controls how much of HBM is reserved for the block pool.
# block_size (default 16) sets how many tokens fit in one physical block.
llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf",
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.90,   # 90% of GPU HBM → KV cache pool + weights
    block_size=16,                  # tokens per physical block
    max_model_len=8192,
)

params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.8, max_tokens=512)

# PagedAttention is invisible at the API level — blocks are allocated and freed
# per token as each request grows and completes.
outputs = llm.generate([
    "Explain paged virtual memory in operating systems.",
    "Write a haiku about transformer attention.",
    "What is the capital of France?",
], params)

for out in outputs:
    print(out.outputs[0].text[:120])
Copy-on-Write for Beam Search and Parallel Sampling
When beam search forks one sequence into N beams, the prefix KV blocks are
rather than copied. All beams point to the same physical blocks for the common prefix. When a beam needs to write a new token to a block that has ref_count > 1, the allocator performs a
: it allocates a fresh block, copies the old block's content, decrements the old block's ref_count, and updates the block table to point at the new block. This means
blocks of GPU memory instead of

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

# Beam search: 4 beams share prefix blocks via COW
llm = LLM(model="meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf")

params = SamplingParams(
    n=4,               # 4 parallel samples (each gets independent block table)
    best_of=4,         # beam-search style: generate 4 and pick best by logprob
    temperature=0.0,   # greedy within each beam
    max_tokens=256,
    use_beam_search=True,
)

outputs = llm.generate(["Summarize the key ideas of the French Revolution."], params)
for candidate in outputs[0].outputs:
    print(f"score={candidate.cumulative_logprob:.3f}: {candidate.text[:60]}...")
vLLM estimates the block pool size at startup by profiling how much HBM the model weights consume, then subtracts that from the
target. The remainder is divided into blocks of size
. You can inspect the computed pool size:

import torch
from vllm import LLM

llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf",
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
    block_size=16,
    dtype="float16",
)

# Internal engine stats
cache_config = llm.llm_engine.cache_config
scheduler_config = llm.llm_engine.scheduler_config

print(f"Block size (tokens): {cache_config.block_size}")
print(f"GPU blocks allocated: {cache_config.num_gpu_blocks}")
print(f"CPU blocks (swap):    {cache_config.num_cpu_blocks}")

# Compute bytes per block manually to cross-check
model_cfg = llm.llm_engine.model_config
num_layers = model_cfg.get_num_layers(llm.llm_engine.parallel_config)
num_kv_heads = model_cfg.get_num_kv_heads(llm.llm_engine.parallel_config)
head_dim = model_cfg.get_head_size()
dtype_bytes = 2  # float16

bytes_per_block = (
    cache_config.block_size * num_layers * 2 * num_kv_heads * head_dim * dtype_bytes
)
total_kv_bytes = cache_config.num_gpu_blocks * bytes_per_block
print(f"KV cache pool: {total_kv_bytes / 1e9:.2f} GB")
print(f"Max concurrent tokens across all reqs: {cache_config.num_gpu_blocks * cache_config.block_size:,}")
When the block pool is exhausted while a batch is in flight, vLLM
the lowest-priority request: it either (a) swaps its blocks to CPU RAM (
reserves space for this) or (b) recomputes the prefix from scratch on resumption. Preemption is rare under well-tuned
settings but visible in the engine logs as
Preempting request due to lack of KV cache blocks
Allocate one block per block_size new tokens
Impossible — contiguous layout differs per request
option extends paging further: completed physical blocks whose token content is identical to a block in a new request can be
(ref_count incremented) without re-running the attention computation. The allocator hashes each block's token sequence and stores it in a
map. This is highly effective when many requests share a long system prompt:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

# Enable prefix caching — blocks from completed requests stay resident
# and are reused for new requests with matching token prefixes.
llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf",
    enable_prefix_caching=True,
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
)

SYSTEM_PROMPT = "You are a helpful assistant specialized in Python.\n\n" + "x" * 3000

params = SamplingParams(max_tokens=128)

# First request: fills prefix blocks from scratch
out1 = llm.generate([SYSTEM_PROMPT + "\nQ: What is a decorator?"], params)

# Second request with same prefix: prefix blocks are cache-hit → faster TTFT
out2 = llm.generate([SYSTEM_PROMPT + "\nQ: What is a generator?"], params)

# The TTFT for out2 should be significantly lower than out1 because
# the long system prompt's KV is already in the block pool.
less waste on short requests, better for highly-variable-length workloads (chat). More block table entries, slight address-translation overhead.
higher GPU memory efficiency for long-context requests (RAG, summarization). Worse waste if most requests are short.
larger blocks are better because a cache hit requires the entire block to match. block_size=16 is a safe default for mixed workloads.
leave at the default (~512) unless you want aggressive preemption tolerance. Swap adds PCIe latency.

The throughput improvement from PagedAttention is best measured by comparing output tokens per second at the same batch size. The gains are largest when requests have unpredictable or highly variable output lengths — exactly the cases where contiguous allocation wastes the most memory:

"""
Throughput benchmark: compare vLLM (paged) against HuggingFace (contiguous).
Run on a single A100 80GB.
"""
import time, torch
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

MODEL = "meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-hf"
PROMPTS = [f"Write a detailed explanation of topic number {i}." for i in range(64)]
MAX_TOKENS = 256

# --- vLLM (PagedAttention) ---
llm = LLM(model=MODEL, gpu_memory_utilization=0.85)
params = SamplingParams(max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS, temperature=0.0)

t0 = time.perf_counter()
outputs = llm.generate(PROMPTS, params)
vllm_time = time.perf_counter() - t0

total_out_tokens = sum(len(o.outputs[0].token_ids) for o in outputs)
print(f"vLLM:  {total_out_tokens / vllm_time:.1f} output tokens/s  ({vllm_time:.1f}s total)")

# --- HuggingFace (contiguous KV) ---
del llm  # free VRAM
torch.cuda.empty_cache()

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto")

t0 = time.perf_counter()
for prompt in PROMPTS:  # HF doesn't easily batch variable-length outputs
    inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=MAX_TOKENS, do_sample=False)
hf_time = time.perf_counter() - t0

# approximate: each prompt averaged MAX_TOKENS new tokens
hf_out_tokens = len(PROMPTS) * MAX_TOKENS
print(f"HF:    {hf_out_tokens / hf_time:.1f} output tokens/s  ({hf_time:.1f}s total)")
print(f"Speedup: {(hf_out_tokens / hf_time) / (total_out_tokens / vllm_time):.2f}x (lower = vLLM faster)")
# Expected output (A100 80GB, Llama-3-8B fp16):
#   vLLM:  ~1800 output tokens/s
#   HF:    ~450 output tokens/s
#   vLLM is ~4x faster on this 64-request batch

vLLM extends PagedAttention to tensor-parallel multi-GPU setups transparently. Each GPU in a TP group holds a shard of the KV heads (as with normal TP), and each GPU maintains its own block pool for its shard. The block tables are replicated across GPUs, but the physical block IDs index into per-GPU VRAM independently:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

# 2-GPU tensor parallel: each GPU holds half the KV heads.
# Each GPU maintains its own paged block pool.
llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-hf",
    tensor_parallel_size=2,          # shard across 2 GPUs
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.90,
    block_size=16,
)

params = SamplingParams(max_tokens=512, temperature=0.7)
outputs = llm.generate(["Explain the attention mechanism in transformers."], params)
print(outputs[0].outputs[0].text[:200])

# Under the hood, each GPU worker runs the paged attention kernel on its
# KV shard, then all-reduces the partial attention outputs across GPUs.
# The block manager on the driver process tracks a unified logical→physical
# mapping, with offsets into each GPU's physical pool.
Out-of-memory at startup, not at inference time:
vLLM pre-allocates the entire block pool upfront. If
leaves too little room for activations, reduce it to 0.80–0.85.
max_model_len limits the block table size:
the maximum number of logical blocks per request is
too high wastes GPU block table memory; set it to your actual 99th-percentile request length.

block hashes are computed over token IDs. If a model's tokenizer produces different IDs for the same text (e.g., due to byte-fallback or BPE instability), prefix cache hits will be missed. Use a fixed tokenizer version.
the last block of each sequence is partially filled. With 1000 concurrent short requests of average length 30 tokens and
, each request wastes ~1 token slot on average — negligible. But with
and 30-token requests, average waste rises to ~34 tokens per request.
CPU swap has PCIe bandwidth cost:

swapping one 16-token block for Llama-3-8B takes ~32 KB × 32 layers = ~1 MB per block at fp16. Moving 100 blocks through PCIe 4.0 (64 GB/s) takes ~1.5 ms — noticeable latency if preemption is frequent.

PagedAttention replaces fixed contiguous KV tensors with a pool of fixed-size physical blocks managed by a free list. A per-sequence block table translates logical token positions to physical block addresses at decode time. The result is near-zero fragmentation, copy-on-write beam search, and block-level prefix caching — each a consequence of the same virtual-memory abstraction applied to GPU KV buffers.
